rateLimitingStep: At neutral pH, intramolecular proton transfer between C1 and C2 (or diffusion for GAP→DHAP)

steps.title: Substrate binds and the Ω-loop closes
steps.description: DHAP (or GAP) binds in the active site with its phosphate anchored by Asn11, Lys13 and the N-terminus of helix α6. Loop 6 (residues 166–176) closes over the active site, sequestering the substrate from bulk solvent and pre-organising the electrostatics. Loop 7 (residues 208–213) also contributes.
steps.notes: Loop closure is essential: open-loop TIM is catalytically inert and leaks methylglyoxal via enediolate decomposition.

steps.title: Glu165 abstracts the pro-R proton from C1 of DHAP
steps.description: Glu165 carboxylate (general base) abstracts the pro-R hydrogen from C1 of DHAP. His95 donates a hydrogen bond to the C2 carbonyl, polarising it. Lys13 stabilises the developing negative charge.

steps.title: cis-Enediolate intermediate
steps.description: A planar cis-enediolate intermediate is formed, stabilised by hydrogen bonds from His95 and Lys13 and by the electrostatic environment of the closed loop.
steps.notes: The enediolate is not released into solvent — loop closure prevents this (avoiding methylglyoxal formation, which would be cytotoxic).

steps.title: Glu165 donates proton to C2
steps.description: The protonated Glu165 delivers the proton back to C2 on the opposite face, producing GAP. Net effect: stereospecific isomerisation with 1,2-proton shift mediated by a single residue.

steps.title: Loop opens, product releases
steps.description: Loop 6 opens, GAP diffuses out, and the enzyme is reset. kcat/KM for GAP → DHAP approaches 10⁸ M⁻¹s⁻¹, within an order of magnitude of the diffusion limit — TIM is a 'kinetically perfect' enzyme.

references.citation: Knowles JR. Enzyme catalysis: not different, just better. Nature 350:121–124 (1991).
Richard JP. A paradigm for enzyme-catalyzed proton transfer at carbon: triosephosphate isomerase. Biochemistry 51:2652–2661 (2012).
Wierenga RK, Kapetaniou EG, Venkatesan R. Triosephosphate isomerase: a highly evolved biocatalyst. Cell Mol Life Sci 67:3961–3982 (2010).
